suddenly you look and you've got 10 or 20 people on payroll, and you realize that a bunch of them are doing the same thing, or there's overlap, and none of them are actually working full time, they're all kind of just doing a little bit of stuff, and nobody knows, there's not clear delineation, or definition about their roles and responsibilities, and just all that eats into your profit, so then another concept in there is managing for profit, you guys and girls know what managing for profit is, if you don't, it's totally cool, curious, yeah, jazz, no, nope, cool, yeah, a lot of people don't, so really the way I would explain it, and I guess that you wanna look at like what you want your profit to be, you have to look at kind of your current numbers ideally, but then you look at what you want your profit margin to be, and then you basically look at like what it takes to get there, and then you make decisions for your business, like management decisions based on like maintaining or achieving that profit margin, so instead of like what a lot, but it sounds really simple, what a lot of people do as they're scaling an offer or starting a business, is they kind of look at the numbers reactively, and they say, okay, if we did this, our margin was 10%, I wanna get to 15%, you know, how do I do that? Well, it's way better to be like, okay, my profit margin would be 20%, like based on that, here's what needs to happen, I need to have this AOV pay this CPA, I can have this many people and this much overhead, I need to be paying this way in merchant processing, I need to do all these things, and if I'm doing these things, then that gets that profit percentage, and then knowing that, you also know like, okay, if I hire people, is that gonna eat into this profit percentage and cut it down?
